sourceSections: Anthropic Certified Architect Exam Structure and Claude Fundamentals
Effective Claude Code Configuration and Prompt Engineering
Designing Robust Tools and Managing Context
Comprehensive Study Plan and Exam Distractor Patterns

sections.lead: The Anthropic Cloud Certified Architect exam has 60 scenario-based multiple-choice questions requiring a 75% score, testing knowledge across four out of six production scenarios.

bullets.label: Claude Core
bullets.text: Claude is a stateless language model accessed via a single HTTP endpoint, `api.anthropic.com/v1/messages`, operating without memory between calls.

bullets.label: Functionality Layers
bullets.text: Claude's functionalities are built as layers, including the Claude API, Agent SDK, Claude Code, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

bullets.label: Exam Focus
bullets.text: Understanding which specific layer handles particular behaviors is crucial for success on the exam.

sections.lead: Claude's stateless nature requires external code for orchestration, using a `call-inspect-execute-append-call` cycle for multi-step tasks.

bullets.label: Orchestration Loop
bullets.text: External code acts as the orchestrating agent loop for multi-step tasks, following a `call-inspect-execute-append-call` cycle.

bullets.label: Multi-Agent Systems
bullets.text: For complex tasks, a "coordinator" Claude spawns smaller "sub-agents" via the Agent SDK's `task` tool, passing specific tasks.

bullets.label: Recovery Strategy
bullets.text: When a sub-agent fails, the correct recovery is a targeted retry of only the failed agent to prevent unintended side effects.

sections.lead: Claude Code's behavior is configured with hierarchical `Claude.md` files and custom slash commands, emphasizing specific prompt rules and structured outputs.

bullets.label: Claude Code Config
bullets.text: Claude Code's behavior is configured using `Claude.md` files, which are stitched into the session's system prompt and operate in a hierarchy.

bullets.label: Custom Commands
bullets.text: Custom slash commands, defined in `.claude/commands`, allow reusable prompts and use YAML front matter for `allowed_tools` and `argument_hint`.

bullets.label: Prompt Rules
bullets.text: Prompt engineering emphasizes specific, mechanically checkable rules like "flag if function exceeds 40 lines" over vague adjectives for clarity.

bullets.label: Structured Output
bullets.text: For reliable structured output, declare the output shape as a tool using `input_schema` and force its use with `tool_choice`.

bullets.label: Message Batches
bullets.text: For large-scale, offline, latency-insensitive tasks, use the message batches API for cost-effective processing.

sections.lead: Robust tool design requires clear MCP descriptions and structured errors; context management, prompt caching, and agent escalation are key.

bullets.label: Tool Descriptions
bullets.text: Effective Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool descriptions must clarify functionality, disambiguation rules, example invocations, and error conditions.

bullets.label: Error Responses
bullets.text: Tools should return structured JSON error responses with fields like `is_error`, `category`, `retryable`, and `retry_after_ms` for intelligent recovery.

bullets.label: MCP Transports
bullets.text: Choose STDIO for tools in the same process (zero latency/auth) and SSE only when a remote server is necessary (HTTP, network latency/auth).

bullets.label: Context Management
bullets.text: To combat the "lost in the middle" effect, extract durable facts into a small "case block" and re-anchor it at the end of the context on every turn.

bullets.label: Prompt Caching
bullets.text: Prompt caching reduces costs for repetitive sections like system prompts by adding a `cash_control_breakpoint` to cache internal states.

bullets.label: Agent Escalation
bullets.text: Reliable agents must know when to escalate by implementing explicit confidence checks and ambiguity detection to trigger human handoff with a structured summary.

sections.lead: A 4-week study plan combines theory, projects, and practice exams, while recognizing five common distractor patterns is crucial for success.

bullets.label: Study Plan
bullets.text: The recommended 4-week study plan involves weekly reading domain guides, building graded projects, and consistent flashcard review.

bullets.label: Practice Exams
bullets.text: Six practice exams serve as checkpoints, with Week 4 concluding with a full dress rehearsal using timed practice exams.

bullets.label: Vague Adjective Trap
bullets.text: Avoid prompts using vague adjectives like "careful" or "thorough"; use numbered, categorical rules instead.

bullets.label: Summarization Trap
bullets.text: Do not summarize long conversations with critical IDs/amounts; extract durable facts into a case block to avoid loss.

bullets.label: Blanket Retry Trap
bullets.text: Do not rerun all sub-agents if one fails after others had side effects; retry only the failed agent.

bullets.label: User claude.md Trap
bullets.text: Don't put team-shared configuration in the user-level `claude.md`; use the project-level file at the repo root.

bullets.label: SSE on Localhost
bullets.text: Pick STDIO for tools running on the same machine as the client, not SSE, to avoid unnecessary network overhead.

sections.lead: Success on the Anthropic Cloud Certified Architect exam requires deep architectural understanding, practical agentic design, and prompt engineering skills.

bullets.label: Core Understanding
bullets.text: Passing requires a deep understanding of Claude's architecture, agentic design principles, and prompt engineering best practices.

bullets.label: Key Skills
bullets.text: Robust tool integration, context management, and reliability patterns are also essential for success.

bullets.label: Preparation Focus
bullets.text: Diligently follow a structured study plan, including hands-on building, and recognize common exam traps to prepare confidently.
